beats.prompt: Quick warm-up from L2 — match each statement to what it describes.

feedback.correct: One positive left you at 50% because the base rate is the anchor. Now add a second clue.
hints: From L2: a single 99% test on a 1% disease.
One row is the headline number; one is the role of the prior.
One test → 50%; base rate → the anchor evidence must overcome.

beats.prompt: Same 1% disease, same 99% test. You test positive twice independently. What is P(disease | two positives)?

options: Still about 50% — a re-test just confirms

Still about 50% — a re-test just confirm.note: Independent evidence multiplies, it doesn't merely re-confirm.

Around 75%.note: That averages the two 50%s. Bayes multiplies the odds instead.

Much higher — near 99%.note: Right — each positive multiplies the odds by 99: 1 → 99 → 99%.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll multiply it out.
Two 50%s don't average to 50%.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on chaining evidence — then continue.

interaction.title: Today's posterior is tomorrow's prior
interaction.body: After the first test, your 50% belief becomes the prior for the next test. If the tests are independent, their likelihood ratios multiply: posterior odds = prior odds × LR × LR.

feedback.correct: Chain it: today's posterior becomes tomorrow's prior; independent ⇒ multiply.
hints: Carry yesterday's answer forward as today's prior.
Independent evidence ⇒ multiply the likelihood ratios.
posterior odds = prior odds × LR × LR.

beats.prompt: Read this contrast between the gambler's fallacy and Bayesian updating — then continue.

interaction.title: 'Never due' vs 'each head is evidence'
interaction.body: Two different objects. Given the coin, the next flip is independent — you are never 'due' for a head. But the heads you've already seen update which coin you're holding. Independence of outcomes is exactly why their likelihoods multiply.

feedback.correct: Never 'due' for the next flip; but the flips you've already seen still update which coin it is.
hints: The gambler's fallacy is about the next outcome.
Bayes is about which hypothesis you hold.
Independent outcomes are exactly why their likelihoods multiply.

beats.prompt: The 1-in-1000 double-headed coin — flip repeatedly and watch the posterior climb. How many heads to pass 50%?

hero.structuralReadout: Each head doubles the odds; it takes 10 straight heads (1024 vs 999) for the 1-in-1000 coin to pass 50%.

feedback.correct: Flip by flip the posterior climbs 2/1001 → 32/1031 → … → 1024/2023: big early jumps, then it crawls past 50% at the 10th head.
hints: Flip repeatedly; each head multiplies the odds by 2.
It's multiplicative in odds, not a fixed +amount in probability.
After 10 heads: 1024/2023 — just over half.

beats.prompt: Same 1% disease, same 99% test. You test positive twice (independently). Compute P(disease | two positives).
beats.interviewNote: Odds-form Bayes is the trader/quant mental model: stack independent evidence by multiplying likelihood ratios (equivalently, adding log-LRs). Two LR-99 positives on prior odds 1:99 give 99:1 → 99%.

feedback.correct: Odds 1:99 × 99 × 99 = 99:1 → 99/100. Two independent positives multiply; they don't average.
hints: Start at prior odds 1:99; each positive multiplies by its likelihood ratio 99.

beats.prompt: Two coins (one fair, one two-headed) — match each number of heads to its exact posterior.

feedback.correct: The two-coin ladder is 2ᵏ/(2ᵏ+1): 2/3, 4/5, 8/9 — big early jumps, then diminishing (not a fixed +amount).
hints: Two coins (fair + two-headed); each head doubles the odds.

beats.prompt: Three lenses converge on the same crossing point — reveal each and see which k is enough.

feedback.correct: All three lenses cross 1/2 at exactly the 10th head.
hints: Reveal each lens.
2^9 = 512 is still under 999.

beats.prompt: Transfer: 1 die in a box of 100 is loaded to always roll a 6; the other 99 are fair. You grab one at random and it keeps rolling 6. What is the smallest number of 6s in a row that makes the loaded die more likely than not?

interaction.scenario: 1 of 100 dice always rolls a 6 (loaded); the other 99 are fair (a 6 with probability \(\tfrac{1}{6}\)). A randomly chosen die keeps rolling 6.

fields.label: Smallest number of consecutive 6s to make the loaded die more likely than not

feedback.correct: Same odds-form update as the double-headed coin, but each roll's likelihood ratio is now \(6\), not \(2\): \[ \frac{P(6\mid\text{loaded})}{P(6\mid\text{fair})} = \frac{1}{1/6} = 6 \] After \(k\) sixes the posterior odds are: \[ \text{posterior odds} = \frac{1}{99}\times 6^k \] The loaded die is more likely once \(6^k > 99\). Checking: \(6^2 = 36 < 99\) (not enough); \(6^3 = 216 > 99\) (enough). So \(k = 3\). At \(k = 3\) the posterior is \(\frac{216}{216+99} = \frac{216}{315} = \frac{24}{35}\) — comfortably over half. A stronger per-trial likelihood ratio (\(6\) vs \(2\)) flips belief in far fewer observations.
hints: Each 6 multiplies the prior odds by the likelihood ratio \(\frac{P(6\mid\text{loaded})}{P(6\mid\text{fair})} = \frac{1}{1/6} = 6\).
Prior odds are \(1:99\); you need \(6^k > 99\). Checking: \(6^2 = 36\) (not enough), \(6^3 = 216\) (enough).
\(k = 3\); at that point the posterior is \(\frac{216}{315} = \frac{24}{35}\) — just over \(\tfrac{1}{2}\).

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: the 1-in-1000 double-headed coin keeps landing heads. What is the smallest number of heads to make the double-headed coin more likely than not?

interaction.scenario: The 1-in-1000 double-headed coin keeps landing heads. (The double-headed coin always shows heads; the fair coin shows heads half the time.)

fields.label: Smallest number of heads to make it more likely than not

feedback.correct: Each head multiplies the prior odds by the likelihood ratio \(\frac{P(H\mid\text{DH})}{P(H\mid\text{fair})} = \frac{1}{1/2} = 2\). After \(k\) heads the posterior odds are: \[ \text{posterior odds} = \frac{1}{999}\times 2^k \] The double-headed coin is more likely when \(2^k > 999\). Checking: \(2^9 = 512 < 999\) (not enough); \(2^{10} = 1024 > 999\) (enough). So \(k = 10\). The posterior at \(k = 10\) is \(\frac{1024}{1024+999} = \frac{1024}{2023}\) — just over half. Evidence compounds in odds, not additively in probability.
hints: Each head multiplies the prior odds by the likelihood ratio \(\frac{P(H\mid\text{DH})}{P(H\mid\text{fair})} = \frac{1}{1/2} = 2\).
You need \(2^k > 999\); checking: \(2^9 = 512\) (not enough), \(2^{10} = 1024\) (enough).
\(k = 10\); at that point the posterior is \(\frac{1024}{2023}\) — just over \(\tfrac{1}{2}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the capstone recap to consolidate what you've shown.

feedback.correct: Independent evidence multiplies — today's posterior is tomorrow's prior — so posterior odds = prior odds × ∏ likelihood ratios.
hints: Two independent positives: odds 1:99 → 1 → 99, i.e. 50% → 99%.
Each head doubles the 1-in-1000 coin's odds; 10 heads to cross 50%.
The whole concept in one line: posterior odds = prior odds × ∏ LRs.
